perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party hereby acknowledges that it is aware that the United States securities laws prohibit any person who has Material non-public information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of such company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities in reliance upon such information.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware that securities laws may discourage persons with non-public information from trading. However, the Receiving Party makes its own trading decisions and the Blueknight Parties do not provide, nor imply by the provision of information, any recommendations regarding the purchase or sale of securities.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the explicit acknowledgment of insider trading prohibitions under US securities laws, creating a potential legal risk. The original text acknowledges awareness, whereas the changed text attempts to downplay liability for potential insider trading activities that may arise from access to non-public information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 promulgated thereunder
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits the employment of any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, the making of any untrue statement of a material fact or the omission of a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or engaging in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. The changed text, by downplaying the acknowledgment of the law and the consequences of insider trading, introduces legal risk, as it might be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent these prohibitions or create ambiguity around the Receiving Party's responsibility to comply with insider trading laws.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the acknowledgment of insider trading prohibitions, creating a legal risk, as the original contract explicitly acknowledged awareness of securities laws, whereas the changed contract attempts to downplay liability. The snippet from 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 explicitly states that it is unlawful for any person to "employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud" or "omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made...not misleading...in connection with the purchase or sale of any security." The change could be interpreted as attempting to circumvent these prohibitions, creating ambiguity around the Receiving Party's responsibility to comply with insider trading laws by downplaying the acknowledgment of the law and the consequences of insider trading.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall (a) keep and maintain any Material Blueknight Non-public Information in strict confidence and (b) not disclose to any other person the fact that Blueknight Non-public Information has been made available to Receiving Party, except, in each case, to the extent that Receiving Party is requested pursuant to, or required by, applicable law or regulation (including any filings required (and disclosures contained therein) under Section 13(d) of the Exchange Act) or by legal process (including by deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, or similar process) to disclose any such information; provided, however, that Receiving Party may disclose such information to such of its representatives who need such information for the purpose of evaluating or assisting with Receiving Party’s investment in Blueknight, it being understood that such representatives shall be informed in advance by Receiving Party of the confidential nature of such information and Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of the provisions of this letter by such representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall (a) keep and maintain any Material Blueknight Non-public Information in confidence and (b) not disclose to any other person the fact that Blueknight Non-public Information has been made available to Receiving Party, except to the extent Receiving Party is requested pursuant to legal process to disclose any such information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the clause that Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of the provisions of this letter by such representatives. By omitting the responsibility clause, the revised text contradicts the intent of protecting non-public information and introduces a legal loophole. This creates a risk that if the Receiving Party’s representatives disclose confidential information, the Receiving Party cannot be held liable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (specifically, sections related to insider trading and disclosure requirements)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 prohibit manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The omission of responsibility for representatives creates a potential avenue for circumventing these regulations. While the Receiving Party is still bound, the lack of explicitly extending liability to their representatives creates a legal ambiguity and weakens the protective measures against misuse of non-public information, increasing the risk of violations of securities laws.

perturbation.justification: The modification removes the Receiving Party's responsibility for breaches by its representatives, which weakens the protection of non-public information. The Law Explanation suggests this omission creates a loophole that could circumvent regulations against the misuse of non-public information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, which the Law Explanation uses as the basis for concern. While Snippet 2 only provides a table of popular names, the Act's existence coupled with the removal of responsibility creates a contradiction that warrants a 'YES'.

perturbation.original_text: Each of Blueknight, the General Partner and Receiving Party agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that each of the parties shall be entitled, without the requirement of posting a bond or other security, to specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any breach of this Agreement. Such remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for a breach of this Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity. In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, the non-prevailing party as determined by a court of competent jurisdiction in a final judgment shall be liable and pay to the prevailing party the reasonable legal fees and expenses such prevailing party has incurred in connection with such litigation, including any appeal therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of Blueknight, the General Partner and Receiving Party agrees that money damages would be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement. In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, each party shall be responsible for their own legal fees and expenses.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the right to seek specific performance or injunctive relief and alters the fee-shifting provision. By stating money damages are sufficient and removing fee-shifting, the revised text contradicts standard practices in enforcing non-disclosure agreements. Specific performance and injunctive relief are typical remedies for breaches of NDAs because money damages are often inadequate to compensate for the irreparable harm caused by disclosure of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General contract law principles regarding remedies for breach of contract (specific performance, injunctive relief)
perturbation.law_explanation: While NY CPLR 7109 relates specifically to replevin actions, the broader implication is that New York law generally recognizes remedies beyond mere monetary damages when those damages are insufficient to make the injured party whole. The modified text, which limits remedies to money damages only, restricts the court's ability to provide the most appropriate and just relief, which is often specific performance or an injunction in cases involving confidential information, thus contradicting the broader equitable principles underpinning contract law in New York and creating a weaker enforcement mechanism for the NDA.

Unique chattel. (a) Injunction, temporary restraining order. Where the chattel is unique, the court may grant a preliminary injunction or temporary restraining order that the chattel shall not be removed from the state, transferred, sold, pledged, assigned or otherwise disposed of until the further order of the court. (b) Judgment enforceable by contempt. Where the chattel is unique, the court, in addition to granting a judgment under section 7108, may direct that a party in possession deliver the chattel to the party entitled to possession. Disobedience of a judgment or order so directing may be punished as a contempt of court. If a party accepts the value of the chattel awarded to him by the judgment, he shall have no claim to the chattel.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for specific performance and injunctive relief, while the changed text limits remedies to money damages and removes the fee-shifting provision. The Perturbation Explanation notes this contradicts standard practices for NDAs, as money damages are often insufficient for irreparable harm from disclosure. The Law Explanation mentions NY CPLR 7109 and implies New York law recognizes remedies beyond monetary damages. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing NY CPLR 7109, explicitly states that where chattel is unique, the court may grant a preliminary injunction or temporary restraining order. This supports the argument that limiting remedies to money damages contradicts the court's ability to provide appropriate relief in cases involving unique assets or confidential information, thus undermining the enforceability of the NDA.
